comps.name: KLCC Retail Lot
comps.notes: Ground floor retail indication near high-footfall corridor.

comps.name: Shah Alam Semi-D Factory
comps.notes: Industrial indication with good highway access.

comps.name: Kajang Development Land
comps.notes: Residential conversion potential noted in indication.

beliefs.claim: For a beginner, real estate can be viewed through two pathways: asset-led investing in a quality property for appreciation, or financing-led use of property to obtain cheaper capital for another purpose.
beliefs.scope: Beginner framework; Malaysia context
beliefs.evidenceFor: The distinction identifies whether the intended economic return comes from the property or from the subsequent use of borrowed funds.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: It omits rental income, value-add operations, development, and other return engines. Financing-led transactions also vary sharply between legitimate equity extraction and artificial-price or undisclosed cash-back arrangements.
beliefs.falsifier: The framework should be revised if it fails to explain the true source of return, debt repayment source, or major risk in a real transaction.

beliefs.claim: A buyer-seller agreement to state a property price materially above both genuine consideration and market value, intended to induce a higher bank loan, is a prohibited-risk pattern rather than a legitimate real estate investment pathway.
beliefs.scope: Malaysia property financing; facts and legal treatment must be confirmed for each case
beliefs.evidenceFor: The lender is induced to advance more funds using a transaction value that buyer and seller know is not genuine. Malaysian Penal Code Act 574 includes cheating, criminal conspiracy and abetment, false documents, and dishonest or fraudulent deeds containing a false statement of consideration as relevant offence categories.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Precise liability depends on the documents, disclosures, lender knowledge, valuation process, fund flows, intent, and professional legal advice.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise only if the lender receives full disclosure of the genuine price, market value, side agreements, and flow of funds and nevertheless formally approves the same financing.

beliefs.claim: For a beginner without substantial cash, low-cost flats and apartments are the preferred starting segment because their low absolute price can make entry and subsequent capital recycling more achievable.
beliefs.scope: Beginner strategy; Malaysian low-cost flats and apartments
beliefs.evidenceFor: Low absolute acquisition and renovation costs reduce the initial capital hurdle and make mistakes smaller in ringgit terms.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Low price can reflect weak occupier demand, poor building management, title or consent problems, limited buyer financing, high auction incidence, or illiquid resale demand. Capital recycling is not evidence that the property itself is sound.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise the preference if comparable higher-priced segments provide materially better risk-adjusted entry, liquidity, and value-creation spreads, or if selected low-cost projects repeatedly fail the mandatory demand, exit, legal, and building gates.

beliefs.claim: The preferred acquisition is the best relative-quality property in a high-demand area, purchased within the price segment that preserves the broadest future buyer pool.
beliefs.scope: Primary property-selection framework
beliefs.evidenceFor: Relative quality creates preference while the right price segment protects affordability, financing access, transaction liquidity, and the size of the future buyer pool.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: The best property may command a premium that removes the investment margin, and apparent high traffic may not translate into residential demand.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if transactions show that lower-quality or narrower-buyer properties consistently deliver superior risk-adjusted exits after controlling for entry price and holding period.

beliefs.claim: Properties with genuine own-stay appeal and multiple credible buyer segments have stronger exit optionality than pure investment units whose next buyers are mainly price-sensitive investors.
beliefs.scope: Residential property selection and exit strategy
beliefs.evidenceFor: Owner-occupiers can value daily-life quality and scarce emotional attributes, while investors provide a second buyer segment when ordinary rent and value remain sensible.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Investor-heavy properties can be highly liquid when yields are strong, and owner-occupier emotion is difficult to predict or quantify.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if exit outcomes show that buyer-pool breadth and own-stay appeal do not improve transaction speed or pricing resilience relative to comparable investor-led properties.

beliefs.claim: EstateLab should not impose one investment priority on all users; it should first identify each user's mandate, then test whether the property is internally consistent with that mandate.
beliefs.scope: EstateLab product philosophy and Stage 1 selection
beliefs.evidenceFor: Users may prioritize capital appreciation, rental resilience, resale liquidity, refinancing potential, or cash flow differently. A professional AI consultant should adapt the analysis to the user's stated objective while preserving decision discipline.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Too much customization may weaken the framework if every preference is accepted without hard constraints or evidence.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if user outcomes show that a standardized priority hierarchy produces better decisions than mandate-specific analysis across different investor types.

beliefs.claim: The founder default property universe for retail investors is residential high-rise, especially condominium and serviced apartment, while flats, old apartments, and high-rise developments older than 10 years are normally avoided unless there is a special-situation reason.
beliefs.scope: Founder default preference; not universal user mandate
beliefs.evidenceFor: Residential high-rise is more accessible to retail investors than commercial, industrial, land, or other property types that require different capital, legal, financing, and operating capability. Older high-rise stock may face management, image, repair, and buyer-liquidity issues.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Some older or lower-cost properties may outperform when bought at the right price, in the right area, with strong management, genuine demand, or specific cash-out logic.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if comparable evidence shows that excluded segments repeatedly produce superior risk-adjusted outcomes for ordinary retail investors under normal investment assumptions.

beliefs.claim: During weak markets, EstateLab must distinguish temporarily mispriced properties from structurally declining properties by testing intrinsic quality, local demand culture, management, buyer depth, and competitive position rather than relying only on numbers.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1 downside protection and market-cycle analysis
beliefs.evidenceFor: In a downturn many assets appear cheap on price, yield, or valuation metrics. Intrinsic quality determines whether value is likely to normalize or continue deteriorating.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Some data-driven investors can protect themselves through conservative yield, cash-flow, and margin-of-safety rules without deeply judging qualitative factors.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if outcome reviews show that quantitative screens alone reliably identify resilient properties across weak market cycles better than combined quantitative and intrinsic-quality analysis.

beliefs.claim: A high-demand area is supported by hard demand engines such as offices, business parks, universities, factories, institutions, and infrastructure that create real rental or occupier need.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1B area selection; Penang, Kuala Lumpur, Selangor
beliefs.evidenceFor: People need to rent near work, study, factories, business districts, and daily-life infrastructure. These demand engines are more durable than marketing hype.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: A demand engine may support only certain tenant segments, and nearby supply, affordability, transport friction, or area image can prevent demand from benefiting the target property.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if areas with apparent hard demand engines repeatedly fail to show occupancy, achieved rent, transaction volume, or buyer depth for nearby comparable properties.

beliefs.claim: Human traffic is the primary area-demand signal, but it must be relevant human traffic that can convert into tenant demand, owner-occupier demand, or buyer depth.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1B area selection and demand validation
beliefs.evidenceFor: Residential value is driven by people who need or want to live near an area, not merely by vehicles or advertised activity.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Some car traffic, commercial traffic, or logistics traffic can indirectly support demand through accessibility or employment even when visible pedestrian traffic is low.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if low-human-traffic areas with strong car access or employment nodes consistently outperform high-human-traffic areas in rental demand and resale liquidity.

beliefs.claim: A growth corridor is credible only when the sponsor or market leader can execute infrastructure, township components, commercial activation, and buyer confidence at scale.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1B growth corridor analysis
beliefs.evidenceFor: Large-scale township value creation depends on execution capability, not sweet talk. Pocket-land or inexperienced developers may not create the ecosystem needed for real corridor growth.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Smaller developers can still succeed in narrow, well-located, or already-infrastructured pockets where they do not need to create the whole corridor.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if smaller or newer developers repeatedly deliver infrastructure, amenities, demand, and resale value comparable to market leaders in similar growth-corridor contexts.

beliefs.claim: The right price segment depends on area-specific buyer affordability, income profile, financing capacity, and target resident quality; RM400,000 to RM500,000 is the founder's current mandate, not a universal rule.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1C price-segment analysis
beliefs.evidenceFor: Each price range has its own target buyer. The founder uses RM400,000 to RM500,000 to balance affordability, resident profile, product quality, and personal installment capacity.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Some investors believe the RM500,000 range can be difficult to dispose, and the best price band may differ sharply by city, project type, buyer income, and financing policy.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if transaction and exit data show that the RM400,000 to RM500,000 band has weaker liquidity or poorer risk-adjusted outcomes than adjacent bands in target areas.

beliefs.claim: A 20% discount to real market value is an attractive founder-default entry signal, while a quality premium around 10% can be acceptable only when scarcity and buyer demand support it.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1C entry-price discipline
beliefs.evidenceFor: Discount protects margin of safety, while rare high-demand layouts in premium areas can justify paying above median when future buyers also value the difference.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Market value may be overstated, discounts can signal hidden defects, and quality premiums can destroy returns if the advantage is cosmetic or not valued by future buyers.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if post-acquisition outcomes show that nominal discounts fail to protect downside or that quality premiums do not improve rental, resale speed, or exit price.

beliefs.claim: The preferred exit profile combines owner-occupier emotional appeal with investor-grade rental return, so the property is not dependent on one future buyer segment.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1D future buyer pool and exit strategy
beliefs.evidenceFor: Owner-occupiers may pay for renovation, comfort, image, and personal fit, while investors can support exit liquidity when the rent still produces a healthy return at the resale price.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Some products are deliberately optimized for one strong segment and may perform well if that segment remains deep and liquid.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if outcome reviews show that narrowly targeted single-segment properties consistently exit faster or at better risk-adjusted prices than comparable multi-segment properties.

beliefs.claim: Developments focused mainly on investment units, especially short-stay or Airbnb-style products, carry higher exit risk because the future buyer pool may consist mostly of yield-sensitive investors.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1D buyer-pool concentration risk
beliefs.evidenceFor: Investor-only products are commonly negotiated by rental yield and lowest price, while owner-occupier emotional demand may be weak or absent.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Investor-focused products can still be liquid where short-stay demand, rules, management, and yield are durable.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if investor-only developments repeatedly show resilient resale prices, transaction speed, and buyer depth through weak markets despite limited own-stay appeal.

beliefs.claim: Freehold residential strata high-rise and freehold individual-title landed properties usually have broad buyer appeal, but tenure preference must be judged by local market culture.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1D buyer pool; Malaysia state and city differences
beliefs.evidenceFor: The founder observes that Kuala Lumpur buyers may accept leasehold for high-quality products, while Penang buyers usually place stronger importance on freehold status.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Leasehold properties in some Penang submarkets or freehold properties with weak product quality may not follow this pattern.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if transaction evidence shows that tenure preference has little effect on buyer depth or pricing after controlling for location, product quality, and remaining tenure.

beliefs.claim: After market and data research, the final site-visit vibe test is a serious own-stay filter: if the founder would not personally stay in the property, the deal should normally be rejected.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1E own-stay appeal and final qualitative due diligence
beliefs.evidenceFor: Future owner-occupiers may react to the same lived experience, neighbourhood feel, project image, security, and arrival quality that cannot be captured fully by numbers.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Some investors may profit from properties they personally dislike if the target tenant or buyer segment values different features.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if repeated outcomes show that failed-vibe properties still achieve strong rent, resale speed, and owner-occupier demand in the intended market segment.

beliefs.claim: Project image, neighbourhood feel, layout, view, surrounding environment, common-area materials, facilities, resident demographics, and security strongly shape own-stay appeal and resale confidence.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1E own-stay appeal and qualitative property quality
beliefs.evidenceFor: These attributes influence whether buyers can imagine living in the development and whether the project feels premium, safe, and respectable.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Some buyers may prioritize price, location, or rental yield over image and feel, especially in highly constrained markets.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if transaction outcomes show that project image and neighbourhood feel do not materially affect resale pricing, transaction speed, or buyer objections compared with quantitative factors.

beliefs.claim: For high-rise projects in the same area, management quality and resident demographics or behavior are primary differentiators of long-term value performance.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1F high-rise project comparison
beliefs.evidenceFor: Management and residents affect maintenance, security, cleanliness, common-area condition, project image, buyer confidence, and whether the building remains desirable after the facade ages.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Location, pricing, layout, view, tenure, and supply can still dominate project performance in some submarkets.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if same-area high-rise outcomes show that management and resident behavior do not materially affect resale value, rentability, or buyer objections after controlling for location and product type.

beliefs.claim: At unit level, layout and view are the highest-priority quality factors, while renovation value-add is real only when it matches tenant or buyer preference.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1F unit-level comparison
beliefs.evidenceFor: Layout and view are difficult to change, while renovation improves value only if it makes the target tenant or buyer prefer the unit over substitutes.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: In some rental markets, price, furnishing completeness, proximity, or immediate availability may matter more than view or layout.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if comparable rental and resale outcomes show renovated units with weaker layout or view outperform better-layout and better-view substitutes on a risk-adjusted basis.

beliefs.claim: Subsale transacted prices and successful auction bid prices should carry more weight than listing prices because they reflect completed buyer behavior rather than seller intention.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1G evidence and valuation discipline
beliefs.evidenceFor: Completed transactions show what buyers actually paid. Listing prices can be inflated, stale, speculative, or disconnected from true demand.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Transacted data may lag current market changes, and auction prices can reflect distressed conditions rather than ordinary market value.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if listing prices consistently predict near-term achievable transaction prices better than recent transacted and auction data in the relevant submarket.

beliefs.claim: A high bank valuation should be treated as a signal to investigate rather than proof of value when it conflicts with transaction evidence, rental support, buyer demand, or project quality.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1G valuation evidence
beliefs.evidenceFor: Bank valuation may rely on stale comparables, optimistic assumptions, unsuitable comparables, or bank-specific financing views, and it cannot repair poor project fundamentals.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Bank valuation can sometimes reveal conservative market value that is not obvious from limited public transaction data.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if high bank valuations repeatedly predict actual resale prices and buyer liquidity better than transaction comparables and rental evidence.

beliefs.claim: EstateLab's key Stage 1 protection is to prevent buying merely cheap property; the target must be valuable, with quality and demand that explain why the discount is an opportunity rather than a warning.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1H rejection rules and beginner protection
beliefs.evidenceFor: Cheap properties can remain cheap or decline further when management, resident behavior, layout, density, project image, or buyer demand are structurally weak.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Deep discounts can still compensate for defects when the buyer has a clear strategy, sufficient margin of safety, and evidence that the weakness is fixable or mispriced.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if outcome reviews show that buying cheap assets without strong qualitative value consistently outperforms buying valuable assets at disciplined prices.

beliefs.claim: Rental demand is real when tenant urgency exists, not merely when landlords advertise high rents.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1I rental demand validation
beliefs.evidenceFor: Urgency from job changes, university admissions, workplace proximity, limited suitable supply, or immediate move-in needs shows that tenants have a practical reason to rent.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Some stable rental markets may not show obvious urgency but still maintain consistent occupancy through broad baseline demand.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if properties without visible tenant urgency consistently achieve fast occupancy and stable rent comparable to urgency-driven locations.

beliefs.claim: For the founder default, a rental property should normally be rejected if rent cannot cover the monthly loan installment.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1I cash-flow resilience; founder default
beliefs.evidenceFor: Installment coverage protects holding power, reduces reliance on personal cash flow, and prevents investors from buying assets that cannot sustain debt service through rent.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: A property with weak rental coverage may still be rational if it has a strong resale, redevelopment, or capital-appreciation mandate and the investor has sufficient holding power.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if repeated outcomes show that properties failing installment coverage produce superior risk-adjusted returns without creating unacceptable holding risk.

beliefs.claim: Negative monthly cash flow is normally rejected for high-rise rental property but may be tolerated up to RM450 per month for landed capital-appreciation assets when the user's mandate and holding power support it.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1I cash-flow exception and mandate matching
beliefs.evidenceFor: Landed appreciation strategies may rely more on long-term capital growth than rental yield, while high-rise rental strategies need stronger monthly holding power.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Negative cash flow can still compound risk if appreciation is delayed, financing costs rise, or the investor's income weakens.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if landed appreciation deals with controlled negative cash flow fail to outperform cash-flow-neutral alternatives after accounting for risk and holding duration.

beliefs.claim: Tenant fit depends on property type, and fully furnished units with appropriate facilities, security, and move-in readiness are more rentable against close substitutes.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1I rentability and tenant profile
beliefs.evidenceFor: The founder observes that university students and white-collar tenants create fewer headaches, while furnishing can make a unit easier to rent than competing units in the same project.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Some tenant segments prefer cheaper bare or partially furnished units, and over-furnishing can reduce return if the rent premium is insufficient.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if comparable evidence shows fully furnished units do not lease faster, attract better tenants, or achieve enough rent premium to justify the additional cost.

beliefs.claim: Increasing newer nearby supply is a major future risk to rental demand, rent growth, tenant quality, and resale competitiveness.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1I rental demand and supply risk
beliefs.evidenceFor: Newer projects can offer fresher facilities, better furnishing expectations, stronger marketing, and more choices for tenants, which may pressure older nearby projects.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: New supply may also validate an area, bring amenities, and increase total demand if absorption is strong enough.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if new nearby supply consistently improves rather than weakens rent, occupancy, and resale performance for existing comparable projects.

beliefs.claim: High-rise projects around 1,500 units and above are a founder-default density warning, and lift capacity or waiting time can become an immediate rejection trigger.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1J supply, density, and lived high-rise quality
beliefs.evidenceFor: High unit count and high plot ratio can create facility crowding, lift queues, security friction, parking issues, and weaker liveability unless the project is well designed and managed.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: High density can be acceptable in prime locations when pricing is attractive and the developer, facilities, lifts, and management quality support the resident load.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if high-density projects above the threshold repeatedly show strong rental, resale, lift performance, resident satisfaction, and maintenance outcomes comparable to lower-density substitutes.

beliefs.claim: The most dangerous competing supply is newer high-rise within the relevant substitute radius that offers similar layout and similar or lower pricing.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1J competition and future supply analysis
beliefs.evidenceFor: Buyers and tenants often prefer newer products when layout, price, facilities, and surrounding package are comparable, which can pressure older projects' rent and resale position.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Older projects may defend themselves through better location, larger size, lower rent tier, freehold status, view, management, or a distinct buyer segment.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if older projects with similar competing new supply consistently maintain rent, occupancy, and resale prices without needing discounts or differentiation.

beliefs.claim: New supply can improve an area when it is part of a coherent masterplan where a capable developer segments products to complement rather than cannibalize each other.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1J township and supply interpretation
beliefs.evidenceFor: Masterplanned supply can add amenities, population, infrastructure, commercial activity, and area confidence while differentiating products by price, layout, and target buyer.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Even masterplanned townships can oversupply similar units if pricing is ambitious or demand absorption is weaker than expected.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if masterplanned new supply repeatedly cannibalizes existing projects despite product segmentation and developer control.

beliefs.claim: Small unsold developer stock below 1% is usually not an issue, while many unsold units or many auctions in one building are project-level warning signals.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1J unsold stock, auction, and distress interpretation
beliefs.evidenceFor: Excess unsold developer units may indicate ambitious pricing or weak demand, while many auctions in one building may reveal earlier bulk purchases, investor concentration, or weak owner holding power.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Individual auctions can result from personal financial issues, and unsold stock may be attractive if discounted and fundamentals are strong.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if projects with high unsold stock or many auctions repeatedly show strong subsequent rental, resale, and owner-occupier demand without lasting pricing pressure.

beliefs.claim: For high-rise property, freehold versus leasehold is not the founder's main deciding factor, while landed property should be freehold under the founder default.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1K title preference by property type
beliefs.evidenceFor: High-rise buyer demand may be driven more by product quality, location, bankability, price segment, and project image, while landed property value is more directly tied to land ownership and scarcity.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Some high-rise markets, especially certain Penang submarkets, may still price freehold status strongly, and some leasehold landed properties can be attractive when location and price compensate.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if transaction outcomes show freehold status materially changes high-rise liquidity or pricing more than product quality and location, or if leasehold landed assets outperform after risk adjustment.

beliefs.claim: Commercial-title serviced residences can be acceptable when protected under HDA, but fully office commercial title is uncomfortable for the founder framework.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1K serviced residence title and bankability
beliefs.evidenceFor: HDA protection may make a serviced residence more understandable and bankable for residential-style buyers compared with ordinary office commercial title.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Commercial title can still carry higher costs, different use restrictions, financing friction, or buyer hesitation even if the project is HDA-covered.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if HDA-covered commercial-title serviced residences repeatedly show weak financing, resale, or buyer demand compared with residential-title substitutes.

beliefs.claim: Caveats and consent delays are major transactionability risks and should pause or reject a deal until the issue is understood and resolvable.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1K legal and transaction risk
beliefs.evidenceFor: A caveat or unresolved consent issue can block transfer, delay completion, affect loan drawdown, and create uncertainty about the seller's ability to deliver clean title.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Some caveats or consent requirements are routine and can be resolved through proper legal process within acceptable timelines.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if caveat and consent-delay cases repeatedly complete safely without delay, added cost, or exit impairment in comparable transactions.

beliefs.claim: Master-title property is not automatically rejected if the developer is solvent, but EstateLab must verify transfer, perfection, bankability, and developer execution risk.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1K master title and title perfection
beliefs.evidenceFor: Developer solvency and ability to complete perfection of transfer or charge are central to whether master-title transactions remain clean and financeable.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Some buyers and banks may still accept master-title properties smoothly in established projects, making the risk manageable when process clarity is high.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if master-title properties with solvent developers consistently show no extra delay, legal friction, financing limitation, or resale discount.

beliefs.claim: Bankability is a core exit-liquidity factor because future ordinary buyers must be able to obtain financing without unusual loan-margin or quota restrictions.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1K financing and resale transactionability
beliefs.evidenceFor: Loan margin quotas, title problems, consent delays, commercial-use ambiguity, and bank reluctance can narrow the future buyer pool even when the property itself looks attractive.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: A long-term cash-flow investor may accept weak bankability when rent is strong enough and the strategy does not depend on near-term resale.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if weak-bankability properties consistently retain liquidity, resale value, and buyer depth comparable to normally financeable substitutes.

beliefs.claim: A high weighted score cannot override unresolved hard stops, weak evidence confidence, or a transaction path that is unclear.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1L final scoring and decision standard
beliefs.evidenceFor: Hard stops such as weak demand, poor management, bad layout, title issues, weak bankability, or unsupported entry price can destroy the thesis even when other criteria score well.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Some investors may consciously accept a hard-stop-like risk under a specialized mandate if the discount, holding power, and evidence are strong enough.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if deals with unresolved hard stops repeatedly produce superior risk-adjusted outcomes without hidden liquidity, legal, or management costs.

beliefs.claim: EstateLab can call a property undervalued only when completed comparable evidence, all-in cost, demand support, and absence of hard-stop explanations all support the discount.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1L undervalued versus cheap-for-a-reason decision standard
beliefs.evidenceFor: A low price is attractive only when the discount is measured against defensible value and is not explained by poor management, weak layout, excessive density, title issues, direct supply threat, or weak buyer financing.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Some mispriced opportunities may lack perfect public evidence and require field judgment before the market confirms the discount.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if properties classified as undervalued under weaker evidence standards repeatedly outperform without showing later hidden defects or transaction friction.

beliefs.claim: Every final property decision should be recorded before outcome with score, sub-scores, confidence, thesis, counter-thesis, evidence, missing evidence, walk-away condition, and kill criteria.
beliefs.scope: Stage 1L decision journal and second-brain learning loop
beliefs.evidenceFor: Recording the decision before outcome prevents hindsight bias, exposes rationalization, and allows EstateLab to learn whether the framework predicted buyer depth, rental resilience, and price defense correctly.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Decision journals add friction and may slow down fast-moving opportunities if the record is too long or bureaucratic.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if lightweight unrecorded decisions consistently produce equal or better learning and fewer repeated mistakes than documented decisions.

beliefs.claim: EstateLab should treat most Malaysian users as retail investors unless proven otherwise, and protect them from confusing loan approval with true investment readiness.
beliefs.scope: Investor mandate and profile classification; Malaysia retail investors
beliefs.evidenceFor: Retail investors often rely on agent narratives, personal affordability, and bank approval without fully testing holding power, tenant demand, title risk, and exit liquidity.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Some retail investors have strong local knowledge, cash reserve, sourcing edge, or professional discipline despite not being institutional investors.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if ordinary retail users consistently demonstrate professional-grade due diligence, reserve planning, and outcome quality without extra protection gates.

beliefs.claim: A dangerous investor profile is high income but low property understanding, because borrowing capacity can mask weak judgment.
beliefs.scope: Investor risk profile and suitability gate
beliefs.evidenceFor: High income can support loan approval and monthly payments, but it does not prove the investor understands product quality, demand, exit buyer pool, title risk, or downside.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: High income can provide a stronger margin of safety if paired with humility, cash reserve, and willingness to follow due diligence.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if high-income low-knowledge investors repeatedly produce strong risk-adjusted outcomes without major avoidable mistakes.

beliefs.claim: Investor strategy fit depends heavily on cash reserve: lower-reserve retail investors should prefer rental holding power, while landed appreciation and negative cash flow require high reserve.
beliefs.scope: Mandate classification; high-rise rental versus landed appreciation
beliefs.evidenceFor: High-rise rental strategies can be safer for retail investors when rent covers installment, while landed appreciation often requires stronger cash reserve to survive weaker yield and longer holding periods.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: A low-reserve investor can still be unsuitable for any property, and some high-reserve investors can still make poor decisions if they lack discipline.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if low-reserve investors repeatedly succeed in appreciation or negative-cash-flow strategies without distress, or high-reserve requirement fails to reduce risk.

beliefs.claim: EstateLab should require at least six months of salary as emergency fund before encouraging a retail investor to proceed.
beliefs.scope: Investor readiness and cash reserve gate
beliefs.evidenceFor: A six-month salary buffer protects against vacancy, repairs, income interruption, unexpected costs, and psychological pressure to make distressed decisions.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Emergency fund adequacy should ideally depend on job stability, family support, debt level, property risk, and size of monthly obligations rather than salary alone.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if users with smaller emergency funds consistently survive property stress without distress, or if six months is inadequate for common downside cases.

beliefs.claim: EstateLab should assume a user's practical property budget is at least 10% lower than stated until income, commitments, cash reserve, and costs are verified.
beliefs.scope: Investor affordability verification
beliefs.evidenceFor: Users may overstate affordability by ignoring transaction costs, renovation, furnishing, vacancy, repairs, interest-rate sensitivity, and psychological comfort.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Some users may state conservative budgets already, and a fixed 10% haircut can be too blunt for all income and debt profiles.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if verified user budgets are usually accurate or conservative without requiring a haircut.

beliefs.claim: A user who wants to purchase without comparing substitutes is likely emotionally chasing the deal and should be slowed down.
beliefs.scope: Investor behavior and cooling-off trigger
beliefs.evidenceFor: Skipping comparison prevents the user from identifying cheaper, better, safer, or more liquid alternatives and increases susceptibility to agent narratives.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Rare opportunities may require speed, but speed is safer only when the investor already has strong market context and pre-built comparison knowledge.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if users who skip substitute comparison consistently make better and faster decisions without higher error rates.

beliefs.claim: Malaysian retail investors commonly over-focus on interest rate while underestimating loan margin, cash buffer, DSR, legal disbursements, vacancy, repairs, and downside survival.
beliefs.scope: Deal structuring and financing; Malaysian retail investors
beliefs.evidenceFor: The founder observes that beginners often judge financing by rate alone even though affordability and survival depend on total structure and full cash cost.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Interest rate can still be material when loan size is large, holding period is long, or cash-flow margin is thin.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if financing mistakes in actual cases are more often caused by rate selection than by leverage, weak cash reserve, valuation error, or ignored costs.

beliefs.claim: For beginners, maximizing 90 percent loan margin merely to avoid upfront cash outlay is unhealthy when rental support is weak or the property is underperforming; 90% may be standard, but EstateLab should treat it as healthy only when property quality, rent coverage, DSR, cash reserve, and stress tests support it.
beliefs.scope: Loan margin and leverage discipline
beliefs.evidenceFor: Higher leverage can improve capital efficiency on a strong rental-supported asset, but it magnifies mistakes on underperforming, weak-rental, or overvalued properties.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Some investors may rationally use high leverage with strong non-property income or alternative capital allocation even when property rent coverage is not ideal.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if high-margin loans on weak or thin-cash-flow properties repeatedly survive downturns without distress or poor exits.

beliefs.claim: Large valuation mismatch should trigger investigation: purchase price far above valuation is usually dangerous, while valuation far above purchase price may be opportunity, stale data, error, or hidden defect rather than automatic rejection.
beliefs.scope: Bank valuation and deal structuring
beliefs.evidenceFor: Bank valuation is a financing input and may conflict with transaction evidence, property condition, title, management, or comparable quality.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: A very high valuation relative to purchase price can still indicate genuine mispricing when the seller is distressed and defects are ruled out.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if large valuation mismatches consistently predict outcomes in one direction without requiring further investigation.

beliefs.claim: A retail investor should retain at least six months of salary as emergency fund after down payment, legal fees, stamp duty, disbursements, renovation, furnishing, and other acquisition costs.
beliefs.scope: Cash buffer and financing readiness
beliefs.evidenceFor: Post-cost reserve protects against vacancy, repairs, income interruption, rate changes, and psychological pressure to sell or refinance under stress.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Reserve adequacy should ideally adjust for job stability, family obligations, tenant risk, loan size, and property condition rather than salary alone.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if six months of salary is repeatedly too high or too low for actual stress cases across user profiles.

beliefs.claim: EstateLab should treat 80% DSR as a danger zone for normal retail investors and should stress test DSR against interest-rate increases, lower rent, vacancy, and repairs before encouraging purchase.
beliefs.scope: Debt-service ratio and financing stress test
beliefs.evidenceFor: High debt-service load leaves little margin for income interruption, rate movement, vacancy, or unexpected costs.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: A high-income investor with very stable income and large liquid reserve may survive higher DSR better than a normal retail investor.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if users at or near 80% DSR consistently handle downside events without distress or forced sale.

beliefs.claim: A financing-led property deal is acceptable only when transparent, lender-aware, property-and-debt survivable, and supported by an alternative use of funds with risk-adjusted return above the full financing cost.
beliefs.scope: Financing-led deals and capital allocation
beliefs.evidenceFor: Financing-led returns depend on both property debt survival and the performance of the capital's alternative use; artificial pricing or misleading documents create legal, banking, and solvency risks.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Some legitimate refinancing or equity-release cases may be conservative when based on defensible value and full disclosure.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise only if undisclosed or artificial-price financing structures can be shown to be legally, bankably, and financially resilient under full downside analysis.

beliefs.claim: For normal Malaysian retail investors, rental unable to cover installment is the main holding stress; RM300 loan-only monthly shortfall may be tolerable, while more than RM500 is a danger zone.
beliefs.scope: Stage 4 holding power; Malaysian retail residential investors
beliefs.evidenceFor: Founder input identifies rent versus installment as the main holding failure point and provides practical shortfall thresholds for normal retail investors.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: True stress depends on income, cash reserve, maintenance fee, debt level, tenant quality, and whether the property is rental-led or appreciation-led.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if actual ownership cases show normal retail investors comfortably surviving larger shortfalls without distress, or if smaller shortfalls commonly force bad decisions.

beliefs.claim: Weak rental should be reassessed after about three months by separating temporary events from structural decline such as poor maintenance, weak JMB, repeated complaints, oversupply, and rent cuts across substitutes.
beliefs.scope: Stage 4 rental operations and post-purchase review
beliefs.evidenceFor: Founder input points to sudden unusual events as temporary, and building maintenance, JMB quality, social media complaints, and incoming supply as structural warnings.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Three months may be too short or too long depending on tenant seasonality, unit readiness, agency execution, and area-specific rental cycles.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if repeated cases show three-month reviews misclassify temporary rental weakness or miss structural decline.

beliefs.claim: Refinancing is acceptable only when equity has built up and rent still covers the higher installment; selling should be considered when an area reaches saturation and new projects struggle to sell at market or below-median prices.
beliefs.scope: Stage 4 refinance, hold, and sell decision
beliefs.evidenceFor: Founder input ties refinancing to equity margin and rental coverage, and ties exit review to area saturation, weak new-project sales, poor maintenance, and underperformance against nearest comparables.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: A strategic refinance or hold decision may still be valid with temporary weak rent if the owner has high reserve and a credible catalyst is evidence-backed.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if refinances without rent coverage consistently improve risk-adjusted outcomes, or if saturation signals do not predict weaker exit conditions.

beliefs.claim: Before buying, EstateLab should require an assumed holding period and target disposal year so investors do not panic-sell or complain about short-term illiquidity after purchase.
beliefs.scope: Stage 4 holding period discipline and pre-purchase planning
beliefs.evidenceFor: Founder input identifies insufficient holding period discipline as a common Malaysian retail investor mistake.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Some exits should happen earlier when hard evidence invalidates the thesis, even if the planned holding period has not ended.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if investors without pre-set holding assumptions make equally disciplined hold or sell decisions under stress.

beliefs.claim: One own-stay property plus one investment property is a useful founder sequence, but EstateLab must define overexposure through portfolio stress rather than a universal property count or available financing quota.
beliefs.scope: Stage 5 property count, financing sequence, and portfolio exposure
beliefs.evidenceFor: The founder links the early two-property sequence to practical own-stay and investment needs, while cash flow, reserve, debt, and correlated exposure determine whether those properties are actually sustainable.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Current loan-margin policy and borrower eligibility vary and must be verified; even one investment property can be excessive for a weak financial profile, while more properties may be manageable for a resilient investor.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if property count or financing quota alone consistently predicts portfolio distress better than debt, reserve, cash flow, liquidity, and risk correlation.

beliefs.claim: A beginner with limited cash should normally buy one affordable resilient property or wait, rather than use multiple cheap properties as a substitute for financial readiness.
beliefs.scope: Stage 5 acquisition sequence for beginners
beliefs.evidenceFor: Multiple cheap assets multiply transaction costs, vacancy, repairs, management workload, low-quality project risk, and reliance on refinancing; lower price does not guarantee lower total risk.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: A disciplined investor may safely own multiple lower-price assets when each has verified demand, rent coverage, management quality, buyer depth, and the combined portfolio retains strong reserve.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if financially constrained beginners repeatedly achieve stronger risk-adjusted results through multiple cheap properties than through one resilient asset without higher distress or refinancing dependence.

beliefs.claim: Local expertise can justify geographic focus, but EstateLab must still measure correlated exposure to the same tenant engine, supply pipeline, price segment, exit pool, and market cycle.
beliefs.scope: Stage 5 geographic and property-type concentration
beliefs.evidenceFor: Familiarity can improve sourcing and judgment, while several properties in the same area can still fail together when demand, supply, access, or financing changes.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Deep local knowledge and genuinely different tenant or buyer segments within one geography may reduce practical risk more than unfamiliar geographic diversification.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if concentrated portfolios with local expertise consistently avoid correlated distress despite shared demand and supply shocks.

beliefs.claim: Cash-out and refinancing are acceptable scaling tools only when based on defensible equity, full lender transparency, stressed rent coverage, adequate reserve, and a clear productive use of funds.
beliefs.scope: Stage 5 refinancing, cash-out, and capital recycling
beliefs.evidenceFor: Equity release can improve capital efficiency for seasoned investors, but it increases debt and can create overleverage when valuation, rent, or the next investment underperforms.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Some low-leverage investors with strong income and liquid assets may safely refinance even when rent does not fully cover the higher installment, but this is not the normal retail default.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if unsupported valuation, maximum equity extraction, or refinance-dependent portfolios consistently survive downturns and outperform without higher distress.

beliefs.claim: EstateLab should block the next purchase until existing operating performance is verified, reserves remain intact, combined debt survives stress, and the next deal works without future appreciation or cash-out.
beliefs.scope: Stage 5 permission to proceed from first property to next property
beliefs.evidenceFor: A first successful purchase can create overconfidence; re-testing the property, user, debt, reserve, and portfolio role prevents loan eligibility and FOMO from replacing independent analysis.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: A rare time-sensitive opportunity may arise before one full tenancy cycle, but proceeding earlier requires unusually strong verified evidence and greater reserve.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if investors who scale before operating proof and combined stress testing repeatedly achieve equal or better outcomes without greater liquidity or debt problems.

beliefs.claim: Higher interest rates weaken property demand through more expensive debt and stronger fixed-income alternatives, while inflation preserves housing need but can shift tenants and buyers toward cheaper products.
beliefs.scope: Stage 6 Malaysian macro demand and affordability
beliefs.evidenceFor: Founder experience observes capital moving toward fixed deposits or bonds and landlords reducing rent for competitiveness when living costs rise.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Local employment growth, supply scarcity, wage growth, cash buyers, and product fit can offset broader rate or inflation pressure in specific submarkets.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if rising financing costs and living costs repeatedly have no observable effect on approval, transaction, rent negotiation, tenant downtrading, or required investor return.

beliefs.claim: New-project sales rate is a useful area-cycle signal only when interpreted with price, incentives, cancellations, financing, rent, resale liquidity, catalyst delivery, and future supply.
beliefs.scope: Stage 6 early growth and market peak detection
beliefs.evidenceFor: The same headline sales rate can represent healthy early absorption or late-cycle speculative demand depending on pricing and buyer quality.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: In transparent projects with verified signed sales and low incentives, sales rate may provide a strong standalone near-term demand signal.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if headline developer sales rate alone consistently distinguishes early growth from market peak across observed Malaysian projects.

beliefs.claim: Vacant possession creates the immediate rental shock, but EstateLab must price credible future supply from launch and construction rather than wait until completion.
beliefs.scope: Stage 6 supply pipeline and vacant possession
beliefs.evidenceFor: Founder experience identifies the first six months and staggered VP batches as the period when competing listings affect rent, while earlier launch and construction evidence makes that future competition foreseeable.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Announced or launched supply can be delayed, cancelled, rephased, or differentiated enough not to compete directly.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if credible launched and actively constructed substitute supply repeatedly has no effect on buyer decisions, rent, vacancy, or resale before and after VP.

beliefs.claim: Rental direction should be inferred from several agents and conversion evidence, not enquiry count alone; first-batch VP rent may be a temporary scarcity premium rather than stabilized rent.
beliefs.scope: Stage 6 rental market direction
beliefs.evidenceFor: Founder experience uses agent enquiries and observes stronger first-batch rental demand followed by supply waves as more VP units are released.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: A highly active specialist agent with full local coverage may provide a reliable early signal even before broad achieved-rent data exists.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if raw enquiry count from one agent consistently predicts stabilized achieved rent and vacancy better than multi-agent conversion and listing evidence.

beliefs.claim: Sales-gallery crowds, developer layouts, and discounts are sentiment clues, but genuine liquidity requires signed transactions, financing approval, low cancellations, and limited incentive dependence.
beliefs.scope: Stage 6 buyer sentiment and market liquidity
beliefs.evidenceFor: Founder experience links gallery traffic, financing concern, layout, unsold inventory, and discounts to buyer behavior, while each signal can be influenced by marketing or product strategy.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: In some launches, gallery traffic and rapid unit selection may provide a reliable immediate lead indicator before transaction records become available.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if crowd size, layout, or headline discount alone repeatedly predicts completed demand without transaction and financing confirmation.

beliefs.claim: EstateLab should encourage crisis buying only when fear creates an evidence-backed discount in a durable asset and the investor has enough holding power; it must reject the idea that every cheap crisis property will recover.
beliefs.scope: Stage 6 downturn timing and permanent impairment
beliefs.evidenceFor: The founder wants to enter when others are afraid, but earlier framework logic distinguishes temporarily cheap quality from properties that become structurally cheaper during a downturn.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Broad market crises can create unusually wide discounts where a diversified basket performs well even with imperfect asset selection, though this is not the normal retail property case.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if indiscriminate crisis purchases consistently recover as well as quality-screened purchases without greater vacancy, management, financing, or exit problems.

beliefs.claim: The founder targets at least 6 percent net ROI, 10 percent first-year appreciation, seven-to-eight-year holding, and exit to owner-occupiers and investors, but EstateLab must define the return formula and separate entry discount from later appreciation.
beliefs.scope: Stage 7 founder decision targets and outcome measurement
beliefs.evidenceFor: The founder provided explicit return, holding, entry-discount, and buyer-pool assumptions for pre-purchase decisions.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: A 10 percent one-year appreciation target may conflict with stagnant conditions and a long holding thesis, while 6 percent net ROI is materially different from the earlier 6 percent gross-yield threshold.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise the targets if completed founder cases show that durable successful properties regularly miss them, or weak properties meet them without producing acceptable long-term risk-adjusted outcomes.

beliefs.claim: Saleability is the founder's main blind spot and must receive a mandatory pre-purchase pre-mortem covering buyer depth, financing, substitutes, objections, time to sell, and liquidity discount.
beliefs.scope: Stage 7 pre-purchase exit and saleability review
beliefs.evidenceFor: The founder repeatedly identifies saleability as the hardest assumption to verify, a risk likely to be underestimated, and the criticism most likely to change a deal decision.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: For an explicitly long-term cash-flow property, immediate saleability may carry less weight, although eventual exit and refinancing liquidity still matter.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if saleability pre-mortems fail to identify meaningful exit differences or do not improve actual selling outcomes across comparable assets.

beliefs.claim: An inferior unit position near refuse facilities, lifts, service areas, noise, or heavy corridor traffic can create a permanent resale penalty that a cheap entry does not automatically compensate.
beliefs.scope: Stage 7 unit-level first-purchase lesson
beliefs.evidenceFor: The founder's first purchase became harder to sell after unit placement was overlooked in exchange for a cheaper entry price.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Some tenant or buyer segments may accept the position when the discount is large, noise and smell are controlled, or layout and view provide stronger advantages.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if inferior-position units repeatedly achieve equivalent rent, selling time, and resale price to better-positioned units after controlling for condition and size.

beliefs.claim: EstateLab should use market data first and site visits to test lived quality, but should investigate disagreement rather than assume either data or intuition is always correct.
beliefs.scope: Stage 7 evidence hierarchy, site visit, and intuition
beliefs.evidenceFor: The founder values reliable data and also finds site-visit vibe essential because lived defects and resident behavior may not appear in transaction numbers.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: In rare cases, a highly experienced local investor may correctly recognize qualitative risk before enough market data exists.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if recorded pre-purchase intuition consistently predicts outcomes better than comparable, rental, management, and supply evidence without confirmation bias.

beliefs.claim: Property profit does not by itself prove a good decision; EstateLab must score process, execution, financial outcome, and luck separately using the thesis recorded before purchase.
beliefs.scope: Stage 7 outcome review and skill assessment
beliefs.evidenceFor: Market appreciation, policy, or an unexpected buyer can rescue a weak process, while unforeseeable events can hurt a disciplined decision.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Repeated realized profit after full costs across different market conditions is strong evidence of skill even when individual causal attribution remains imperfect.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if weak-process decisions consistently outperform disciplined decisions across many independent cases without greater downside or reliance on favorable external events.

beliefs.claim: Following the complete EstateLab framework reduces avoidable mistakes but cannot eliminate post-purchase errors or uncertainty, so every material outcome still requires review.
beliefs.scope: Stage 7 framework governance and post-purchase learning
beliefs.evidenceFor: Property outcomes remain exposed to incomplete information, concealed defects, shocks, tenant behavior, management deterioration, and execution even after disciplined due diligence.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: A mature framework may eliminate a large share of recurring preventable errors, making remaining failures mostly external or low-frequency.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise only if complete framework compliance repeatedly eliminates incorrect assumptions, execution mistakes, and unexpected risks across a meaningful set of cases.

beliefs.claim: A poor-quality property in a strong location may remain rentable when priced low enough, but healthy occupancy does not prove healthy net return, tenant quality, or saleability.
beliefs.scope: Stage 7 testable founder belief on location versus property quality
beliefs.evidenceFor: The founder believes tenants may prioritize affordable rent and location over property quality, preserving rental demand.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: Poor management and build quality can require rent discounts, increase repairs and turnover, narrow tenant quality, and weaken resale even in a strong location.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise upward if poor-quality properties repeatedly match better-managed substitutes on achieved net return, vacancy, turnover, tenant quality, and resale; revise downward if price concessions preserve occupancy but not total performance.

beliefs.claim: Beliefs should be updated by evidence quality rather than raw count: one severe credible counterexample reopens a belief, three independent in-scope cases trigger formal review, and five consistent cases normally require revision.
beliefs.scope: Stage 7 belief confidence and learning protocol
beliefs.evidenceFor: This standard prevents one anecdote from casually overturning a rule while still allowing a highly relevant severe case to expose a dangerous blind spot.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: The appropriate threshold depends on base rate, sample size, causal strength, and severity; some beliefs require far more than five observations.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if this protocol repeatedly changes beliefs too quickly, preserves false beliefs too long, or fails to distinguish high-quality from weak counterevidence.

beliefs.claim: The investor should make and own the final decision, but self-belief and external advice must face the same evidence, comparison, and downside tests.
beliefs.scope: Stage 7 independent judgment and responsibility
beliefs.evidenceFor: Only the investor fully knows their financial position and goals, while both personal confidence and outside influence can create bias.
beliefs.evidenceAgainst: A specialist with superior information may deserve greater weight in a narrow technical area, though the investor still owns the final decision.
beliefs.falsifier: Revise if automatic self-trust or automatic external deference consistently produces better decisions than evidence-tested independent judgment.
